findings.issue: rm -rf block-list bypass: the critical-path check uses string replacement to strip flags and the path, then compares the remainder against a short allow-list. This approach has at least six bypass classes: (1) double spaces ('rm  -rf /') survive .replace('-rf','') because the token comparison expects exact equality after trim; (2) env-var expansion ('rm -rf $HOME/' passes because $HOME is not in the literal list); (3) unicode lookalikes in the path (e.g. '/\u200b') survive trim; (4) quoted paths ('rm -rf "/"') — the quote characters are not stripped so target becomes '"' not '/'; (5) flag reordering ('rm -fr /') is handled but 'rm -f -r /' is not — the compound flag '-fr' is replaced but '-f -r' leaves '-r /' after the inner replace pass, making target == '-r /' which does not match '/'; (6) shell metacharacters can split the token ('rm -rf / && true' — after stripping, target becomes '&& true').

rm -rf block-list bypass via compound flag split: 'rm -f -r /' is not blocked. The check fires on .contains('-rf') || .contains('-fr'), but 'rm -f -r /' contains neither '-rf' nor '-fr' as substrings. The individual-flag remove pass does strip '-r' and '-f' but the path token extraction is already wrong (see finding above). Combined, 'rm -f -r /' falls through to the High-risk path and only warns, not blocks.

ToolCall is cloned unconditionally on every tool execution, even when no name repair is needed. The clone allocates a new String for name, a new String for id, and deep-copies the arguments Value (which can be a large JSON tree). This clone happens before the tool is dispatched, and then tool_call.arguments is cloned AGAIN inside ToolRegistry::execute (line 203 of registry.rs: tool.execute(tool_call.arguments.clone())) and once more in each retry attempt. For a busy agent loop executing dozens of tool calls, this is three heap allocations of the arguments tree per invocation.

load_project_instructions() and load_project_instructions_with_config() use std::fs (blocking) throughout: fs::canonicalize, fs::read_dir, fs::read_to_string, fs::create_dir_all (in check_plan_mode_tool), path.is_dir(), path.is_file(). These are called from build_system_prompt_suffix() at line 203 of stack_run.rs, which is inside an async fn that holds an RwLock guard (.read().await). Blocking the async executor while holding a lock prevents other tasks from making progress and can cause priority inversion in the TUI event loop.

Three tokio::spawn fire-and-forget calls in set_thinking_level() (line 548), cycle_thinking_level() (line 566), and set_mode() (line 637) spawn tasks that call async methods on AgentStack but their JoinHandles are dropped immediately. If the AgentStack methods fail (e.g., lock poisoned, channel closed), the error is silently discarded. More critically, if the TUI shuts down while these spawns are in flight, the spawns may race against teardown and attempt to use a partially-dropped stack.

tokio::spawn for codebase indexing (line 141) drops the JoinHandle. If the indexing task panics (e.g., walkdir on a directory with permission errors), the panic is silently absorbed by the runtime. There is no way to know the index was never built, and subsequent codebase_search calls will silently return empty results from the uninitialized RwLock<Option<Arc<CodebaseIndex>>>.

From<std::io::Error> converts to AvaError::IoError(err.to_string()), discarding the std::io::ErrorKind. This means callers cannot match on the kind (NotFound, PermissionDenied, WouldBlock, etc.) to make retry decisions or show differentiated UI. Any code that does .map_err(AvaError::from)? on an io::Error loses the ability to distinguish 'file not found' from 'permission denied'. The same pattern applies to From<serde_json::Error> losing the error category (io vs syntax vs data).

AvaError has 10 string-payload (legacy) variants and 11 structured variants, making the dual-personality doc comment accurate but the boundary unclear to callsites. The structured ToolExecutionError and legacy ToolError coexist, and the is_retryable() logic does not include ToolError (only ToolTimeout) — so any tool error raised via the legacy variant is non-retryable even if it represents a transient failure. Similarly, ToolError is categorised as Tool but so is ToolExecutionError; pattern matching across both is verbose.
findings.recommendation:
Document a deprecation timeline for the 10 legacy variants with a lint (clippy::deprecated) to prevent new code from constructing them. Add a migration table in a doc comment. At minimum, update is_retryable() to check ToolError for known transient substrings to prevent silent non-retry of transient tool failures:

```rust
AvaError::ToolError(msg) if is_transient_error(msg) => true,
```

findings.issue: curl/wget pipe-to-shell detection uses substring matching on '| sh' and '| bash' but misses: '|sh', '|bash' without space (handled), but also '| zsh', '| fish', '| dash', '| ksh', '| sh -c', and heredoc-based execution patterns. An attacker-controlled command could use '| /bin/sh' or '| python3 -c'.

In ToolRegistry::execute, tool_call.arguments is cloned before each retry attempt (lines 203, 224, 256). Since arguments is a serde_json::Value which may be a large nested object, each retry allocates a full deep copy. The retry loop executes up to MAX_RETRIES times per tool call.

spawn_credential_command and several other spawners (spawn_commit_prep at line 162, spawn_provider_connect_load at line 187, spawn_session_list_load at line 202) all drop their JoinHandles. These are fire-and-forget with error reporting via the app_tx channel. The pattern is acceptable since they report completion via AppEvent, but there is no timeout — a slow disk or network operation will leave these tasks running indefinitely with no cancellation path. If the TUI exits while a spawner is in flight, the spawned task holds a clone of app_tx which prevents the channel from closing, potentially delaying shutdown.

contextual_instructions_for_file() is called from execute_tool_call_timed() on every successful 'read' tool invocation (tool_execution.rs line 285). It performs multiple synchronous fs::read_to_string calls inside the async executor. While typically fast for local files, this can add latency on network filesystems or when the agent reads many files in a parallel batch (the read-only parallel execution path).
